food_name: Ebi Tempura (Battered Deep-Fried Tiger Prawns with Tentsuyu Dipping Sauce)

aliases: Udang Goreng Tepung Tempura Jepun (Ebi Tempura)

malay: Udang Goreng Tepung Tempura Jepun (Ebi Tempura)

region_of_origin: Japan (technique derived from Portuguese missionaries, 16th century); widely served in Singapore Japanese restaurants
food_subgroup: Tiger prawns deep-fried in light egg-water-flour tempura batter, served with tentsuyu dipping sauce (dashi, soy sauce, mirin) and grated daikon

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Ebi Tempura. Closest parent is a deep-fried battered seafood dish. No URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tiger prawns in thin tempura batter (egg, water, flour) deep-fried, served with tentsuyu dipping sauce (dashi, soy, mirin). FoodEx2 code A0GPF used as closest parent for battered/breaded crustacean preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for ebi tempura as a composite dish. A0GPF (crustaceans, battered/breaded) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for ebi tempura as a prepared dish.

serving.description: 4 pieces ebi tempura with tentsuyu dipping sauce
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 4 pieces of tiger prawn tempura with tentsuyu dipping sauce, totalling approximately 160g (120g prawns in batter plus 40g dipping sauce).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Shrimp, mixed species, cooked, breaded and fried (FDC ID 175180) combined with HPB Singapore Foodo composite data for ebi tempura
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for breaded and fried shrimp (FDC ID 175180) as the closest approved-source equivalent for tiger prawn tempura. Values cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo composite data for ebi tempura. Tentsuyu dipping sauce sodium contribution (~185mg/100g composite) incorporated into the sodium figure. The composite per-100g values reflect the full dish (120g battered prawn + 40g dipping sauce per 160g serving). Selenium value (38 mcg/100g) is consistent with USDA data for tiger prawns (Penaeus monodon), which are among the highest-selenium crustaceans.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of wheat-flour-based tempura batter — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (wheat flour batter, deep-fried)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ebi tempura as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 58 (medium) is estimated from the closest analogous food: wheat-flour-based light batter, deep-fried, which the University of Sydney GI Database places in the medium GI range (~55–65) for refined wheat flour preparations with fat and protein co-ingestion. The high prawn protein content and fat from frying oil lower the effective GI relative to plain wheat flour (GI ~70). Glycemic load per 160g serving = (58 × 18.9g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 10.9 (low GL). This is one of the lower-GL deep-fried Japanese dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 445mg per 100g; a 160g serving delivers approximately 712mg sodium, primarily from tentsuyu dipping sauce (dashi and soy sauce)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit tentsuyu dipping sauce consumption or request a reduced-sodium version, as the dipping sauce contributes the majority of sodium in this dish; the prawns themselves contribute approximately 185mg sodium per 100g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (58) from wheat-flour tempura batter; however, glycemic load per serving is low (10.9) due to moderate carbohydrate content (18.9g per 160g serving) buffered by high prawn protein (21.6g) and dietary fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ebi tempura is acceptable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions, as the low glycemic load (GL 10.9 per serving) and high protein content from tiger prawns help moderate post-prandial glucose response; portion control and limiting the mirin-containing dipping sauce (which adds simple sugars) are recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 128mg per 100g (205mg per 160g serving) from tiger prawns; saturated fat is low at 1.2g per 100g; polyunsaturated fat 3.2g per 100g from frying oil; sodium 445mg per 100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tiger prawn tempura has low saturated fat (1.2g/100g) and beneficial polyunsaturated fat (3.2g/100g) from vegetable frying oil, but the dietary cholesterol (128mg/100g) and sodium (445mg/100g) warrant moderation for cardiovascular disease patients; the exceptional selenium content (38mcg/100g) provides antioxidant benefit relevant to cardiovascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Exceptional selenium (38mcg/100g; 61mcg per 160g serving = 111% of RDA) from tiger prawns; vitamin D 1.5mcg/100g; vitamin B12 1.5mcg/100g; polyunsaturated fat 3.2g/100g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ebi tempura is clinically notable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its exceptional selenium content (38mcg/100g; 111% of RDA per serving), which supports antioxidant defence and may modulate inflammatory pathways, combined with vitamin D and beneficial polyunsaturated fats from the frying oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ebi Tempura (エビ天ぷら) is the most iconic tempura preparation in Japan and one of the most popular Japanese dishes served in Singapore. Large tiger prawns (Penaeus monodon) are dipped in a minimal, ice-cold egg-water-wheat-flour batter and deep-fried at approximately 180°C in fresh vegetable oil, producing a coating that is almost transparent, crackingly crisp, and remarkably light. The dish is served with tentsuyu dipping sauce — a warm blend of dashi (fish stock), soy sauce, and mirin — alongside grated fresh daikon radish, which aids digestion of fried food. The tempura technique was introduced to Japan by Portuguese Jesuit missionaries in the 16th century during the Ember days of Lent ('Tempora' in Latin), and Japanese chefs refined it over centuries into a culinary art form distinguished by its restraint: minimal batter, clean oil, and immediate service. Nutritionally, ebi tempura is distinguished from Western deep-fried preparations by significantly lower oil absorption due to the thin batter and high frying temperature. Per 100g, it provides 175 kcal, 13.5g protein, 8.5g fat (only 1.2g saturated), and 11.8g carbohydrate. The glycemic load per standard 160g serving is low (GL 10.9) despite a medium GI (58), making it one of the more glycaemically moderate deep-fried Japanese dishes. Tiger prawns are among the highest-selenium crustaceans, providing 38mcg selenium per 100g (61mcg per serving, 111% of RDA), with clinical relevance for antioxidant defence and inflammatory modulation. Sodium is moderate-high at 445mg/100g, primarily from the tentsuyu dipping sauce. Cholesterol is 128mg/100g from the prawns. The dish is halal and dairy-free but contains crustacean, gluten, egg, and soy allergens.
common_user_queries: ebi tempura vs regular fried prawn calories

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are cross-referenced between USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (breaded and fried shrimp, FDC ID 175180) and HPB Singapore Foodo composite data for ebi tempura. Selenium value is consistent with published USDA data for Penaeus monodon. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for wheat-flour-based battered fried preparations, clearly documented. All mandatory multilingual names are present including Japanese (mandatory for Japanese cuisine) and Hindi (included for Singapore context). Ontology uses a defensible closest-parent FoodEx2 code with honest gap notes.
notes: water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite ebi tempura dish (battered prawn plus dipping sauce) is not directly reported in USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175180) or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2026-04-16; moisture varies significantly with frying technique and batter thickness, making a reliable composite figure unavailable from approved sources. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (3.2g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central fatty acid profile for breaded fried shrimp and vegetable frying oil composition; these are not independently verified for tiger prawn tempura specifically and are documented as adapted values. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for wheat-flour batter deep-fried preparations; no direct GI measurement for ebi tempura as a composite dish exists in the database. Sodium of 445mg/100g reflects the composite dish including tentsuyu dipping sauce; prawn-only sodium is approximately 185mg/100g. Two portions are listed in the input: 160g (verified, default) and 150g (GrabFood platform); the 160g verified portion is used as the canonical serving for GL calculation. Hindi local name included as this dish is widely served in Singapore where Hindi is a recognised community language; transliteration एबी टेम्पुरा (जापानी तली हुई झींगा) provided as the closest natural Hindi rendering.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is precise canonical English. Japanese local name エビ天ぷら is present (mandatory for Japanese cuisine). Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names all present for Singapore multilingual context. Romanized form Ebi Tenpura included.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal 175.0, protein_g 13.5, fat_g 8.5, carbohydrate_g 11.8 — all present, numeric, non-zero. nutrient_source cites USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175180) adapted with HPB Singapore Foodo composite data. Source match type documented as adapted with full explanation.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 0.3, sodium_mg 445.0, saturated_fat_g 1.2, sugar_g 2.8 — all present as numeric values, plausible and supported.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 58 (medium), gi_category medium, glycemic_load_per_serving 10.9, gi_source references University of Sydney GI Database as analogous estimate for wheat-flour batter deep-fried preparation. Estimation basis fully documented. GI is not zero and not null.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodEx2 code A0GPF used as closest parent for battered/breaded crustacean preparation with honest gap note. FoodOn URI null with gap note explaining no specific concept exists. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well above 200-character minimum. Covers food identity, preparation method, cultural history, taste/texture, clinical relevance (selenium, GI/GL, sodium, cholesterol), key nutrients, allergens, and dietary flags.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate, sodium from tentsuyu), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate, medium GI but low GL, protein buffering), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate, low saturated fat but cholesterol and sodium). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is 'Type 2 Diabetes' as required.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases flat array contains 18 entries covering English variants, Japanese script (エビ天, エビ天ぷら, エビ天ぷら (Ebi Tenpura)), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, Chinese simplified and traditional, and romanized forms. aliases_by_language is fully populated. Self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language values appear verbatim in flat aliases array — verified: Ebi tempura, ebi tempura, Ebi Tempura, Japanese fried prawn, shrimp tempura, tiger prawn tempura, prawn tempura, Japanese battered prawn, tempura prawn, 炸虾天妇罗, 炸蝦天婦羅, Udang Goreng Tepung Tempura Jepun (Ebi Tempura), ஜப்பான் டெம்பூரா இறால், एबी टेम्पुरा (जापानी तली हुई झींगा), エビ天ぷら, エビ天, エビ天ぷら (Ebi Tenpura), Ebi Tenpura, ebi tenpura — all present in flat aliases.
